name: Discount and Marked Price — What You Really Pay
whatIsIt: Shops print a MARKED PRICE (MP, the tag price) and then offer a DISCOUNT — a percentage OFF the marked price. The discount is always calculated on the MP, and the SELLING PRICE (what you pay) is SP = MP − discount = MP × (1 − d/100). The shopkeeper's profit or loss is then judged against the COST PRICE, not the marked price. Two discounts one after another (successive discounts) do NOT simply add.
whyNeeded: Every sale, festival offer and 'buy-one-get-one' hides this maths. Discount and marked price are core SSC, Bank and Railway aptitude topics and everyday money sense — they stop you being fooled by a big '50% + 20% off!' sign.
realLifeUses: A ₹2000 jacket marked down 25% in a sale
A shopkeeper marking up then discounting to still make profit
Coupon codes that take a percent off your cart
SSC / Bank / Railway quantitative aptitude questions

vocabulary.meaning: The printed / tag price before any discount.
A percentage taken OFF the marked price (discount = MP × d/100).
What the customer actually pays: SP = MP × (1 − d/100).
Two discounts applied one after the other; they do not add — the second is on the reduced price.

story.title: The Real Price of the Shirt
story.text: A shirt is marked ₹800 with '25% off'. 'So I pay ₹775?' guesses Farhan. The shopkeeper laughs: 'The percent comes off, not the rupees. 25% of ₹800 is ₹200, so you pay 800 − 200 = ₹600.' Then a second tag: 'Extra 10% off at the counter.' 'That's 35% total, so ₹520?' 'No — the 10% is on the already-reduced ₹600. 10% of 600 is ₹60, so ₹540. Two discounts never simply add up.'
story.extractedProblem: A shirt marked ₹800 gets 25% off, then a further 10% off at the counter. What is the final price?

visual.caption: Discount is a percent of the marked price. Selling price = MP × (1 − d/100). A second discount applies to the already-reduced price, so successive discounts never simply add.

teachingGallery.title: SP = MP × (1 − d/100)
teachingGallery.note: Take the percent off the marked price. Successive discounts multiply the leftover fractions.

standardMethod.summary: Discount = MP × d/100; SP = MP − discount = MP × (1 − d/100); for two discounts, multiply the leftover fractions.
steps: Selling price SP = MP − discount = MP × (1 − d/100).
For successive discounts, multiply: SP = MP × (1 − d₁/100) × (1 − d₂/100).
Judge profit/loss against the COST price, never the marked price.

alternateMethods.name: Leftover-fraction method
alternateMethods.whenToUse: Fastest for one or more discounts.
steps: A d% discount means you PAY (100 − d)% = the leftover fraction.
Multiply the leftover fractions and by the MP.

workedExamples.problem: A bag is marked ₹1,500 with a 20% discount. Find the selling price.
A ₹1,000 item has two successive discounts of 10% and 20%. Find the final price.

trickPractice.trick: You pay (100 − d)% of the marked price
trickPractice.intro: Turn the discount into the leftover you pay, then multiply.

commonMistakes.fix: The second discount is on the already-reduced price, so they never simply add. Multiply the leftover fractions: 0.9 × 0.8 = 0.72, i.e. 28% off — not 30%.

commonMistakes.mistake: Subtracting the discount PERCENT from the price as rupees (₹800 − 25 = ₹775).
commonMistakes.fix: The discount is a PERCENT of the price, not rupees. 25% of ₹800 is ₹200, so you pay ₹600. Always take the percent OF the marked price.

challenge.q: Two successive discounts of 20% and 25% on ₹1000 — pay (₹)?
Successive discounts of 10% and 10% equal a single discount of…

teachBackPrompt: Teach a friend why '10% + 20% off' is not 30% off. Use a ₹1000 item, apply 10% then 20% step by step to reach ₹720, and show that the single equivalent discount is 28%.

revisionCard.summary: Discount is a % of the MARKED price. SP = MP × (1 − d/100); you pay (100 − d)%. Successive discounts multiply the leftover fractions (0.9 × 0.8 = 0.72 = 28% off, not 30%). Profit/loss is judged against COST price, not marked price.

gameMission.title: Sale Day Showdown
gameMission.brief: Beat the tricky sale tags! Turn every discount into what you actually pay, chain successive offers correctly, and never fall for 'the percents just add up'.

realLifeProject: Find two real sale tags (or make them up): one 'flat 40% off' and one '30% + 15% off'. Work out the final price of each on a ₹1,000 item and decide which is the better deal.

rememberIt.hook: You pay (100 − d)% of the marked price
rememberIt.unpack: SP = MP × (1 − d/100). Successive discounts multiply, never add. Profit is vs cost price.

formulas.name: Selling price
formulas.whenToUse: One discount off the tag price.

studentQuestions.q: Why don't two discounts add up?
studentQuestions.a: The second discount is taken on the already-reduced price, so it removes less money than if it were applied to the full marked price.

studentQuestions.q: Is a big marked price a good deal?
studentQuestions.a: Not by itself — shops often mark up first, then 'discount'. What matters is the final selling price versus the true value.
